name: Frontend Performance — Minimize Bytes & Requests
description: Minimize page weight and HTTP requests. Lazy-load non-critical resources. Eliminate render-blocking scripts and stylesheets.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template: ## Frontend Performance ### Core Rules - Minimize total page weight (images, JS, CSS) - Reduce HTTP requests (combine, inline critical, defer rest) - No render-blocking JS in `` — use `defer` or `async` - Critical CSS inline; non-critical CSS deferred ### LCP Target: < 2.5s ```html ``` ### Script Loading ```html ``` ### Bundle Budget | Page Type | JS (gzipped) | CSS | |-----------|-------------|-----| | Landing | < 150kb | < 30kb | | App page | < 300kb | < 50kb | | Microsite | < 80kb | < 15kb | ### Checklist - [ ] All images have explicit width/height - [ ] Hero image has fetchpriority="high" - [ ] No render-blocking resources - [ ] JS loaded with defer/async - [ ] Bundle within budget - [ ] LCP < 2.5s verified

name: Testing & QA Ownership — Developer Owns Quality
description: Developers are responsible for testing their own code. QA is a safety net, not a primary quality gate. Test at unit, integration, and E2E levels.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## Testing & QA Ownership

### Principle
Developers own quality. QA catches what you miss — QA is not your test strategy.

### Test Pyramid
```
         /E2E\
        /-----\
       /Integr.\
      /---------\
     /   Unit    \
    /-------------\
```
- **Unit**: individual functions, hooks, utilities (fast, isolated)
- **Integration**: API endpoints, component interaction
- **E2E**: critical user flows (auth, checkout, onboarding)

### Minimum Coverage: 80%

### Mandatory Test Cases
- Happy path works
- Error states handled
- Edge cases (empty list, max length, null)
- Accessibility (keyboard nav, screen reader)

### Frontend Test Example
```ts
import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/react';
import userEvent from '@testing-library/user-event';

test('submit button disabled when form empty', async () => {
  render(<LoginForm />);
  expect(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })).toBeDisabled();
  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText(/email/i), 'user@example.com');
  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText(/password/i), 'secret');
  expect(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })).toBeEnabled();
});
```

### Checklist
- [ ] Tests written before or alongside code (TDD)
- [ ] Coverage >= 80%
- [ ] All three test levels present
- [ ] CI runs tests on every PR
- [ ] Flaky tests fixed within 1 sprint

name: Source Code vs Build Artifacts
description: Commit source files only. Never commit minified files, compiled output, or generated artifacts to version control.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## Source Code vs Build Artifacts

### Rule: Never commit generated files
```gitignore
# .gitignore additions
dist/
build/
*.min.js
*.min.css
*.css.map
*.js.map
.next/
out/
```

### Commit: source only
```
src/
  styles/
    main.scss     ✅ commit
    components.scss ✅ commit
  scripts/
    app.ts        ✅ commit
dist/
  main.min.css    ❌ do not commit
  app.bundle.js   ❌ do not commit
```

### SCSS → CSS Pipeline
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "build:css": "sass src/styles/main.scss dist/main.min.css --style compressed",
    "build:js": "tsc && rollup -c",
    "build": "npm run build:css && npm run build:js"
  }
}
```

### Why
- Build artifacts drift from source (someone edits dist directly)
- Merge conflicts in minified files are unsolvable
- CI regenerates artifacts — committing them is redundant

### Checklist
- [ ] `dist/` and `build/` in .gitignore
- [ ] No `.min.js` or `.min.css` in repo
- [ ] CI runs build step before deploy
- [ ] README documents build command

name: DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself
description: Every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous representation. Eliminate duplication through abstraction — utilities, hooks, components.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Separation of Concerns — Structure/Presentation/Behaviour
description: Keep HTML (structure), CSS (presentation), and JavaScript (behaviour) in separate layers. Don't mix inline styles, logic in templates, or visual state in JS.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Write Code to Be Read (KISS)
description: Code is read far more than written. Optimise for the next developer's comprehension. Keep It Simple and Self-Documenting.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Commenting Guidelines — Why Not How
description: Comments should explain WHY something is done, not WHAT the code does. Well-named code is self-documenting. Comments that describe the how are noise.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: File Naming Conventions — Hyphens, No Whitespace
description: Use hyphens as word separators in file names. No whitespace. React components use PascalCase, hooks use camelCase with use prefix, utilities use kebab-case.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## File Naming Conventions

### Rules
| File Type | Convention | Example |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| React components | PascalCase | `UserCard.tsx`, `LoginForm.tsx` |
| Custom hooks | camelCase with use prefix | `useFetch.ts`, `useAuth.ts` |
| Utilities/services | kebab-case | `format-date.ts`, `api-client.ts` |
| Test files | `{name}.test.ts` | `UserCard.test.tsx` |
| CSS modules | `{Component}.module.css` | `UserCard.module.css` |
| Config files | kebab-case or dotfiles | `tailwind.config.ts`, `.eslintrc.js` |
| Route files (Next.js) | kebab-case or `page.tsx` | `about-us/page.tsx` |

### Anti-patterns
```bash
# WRONG
UserCard.Component.tsx  # double suffix
usercard.tsx           # lost PascalCase
User Card.tsx          # space in filename
my_component.tsx       # underscores
FETCH_USERS.ts         # screaming snake

# CORRECT
UserCard.tsx
useFetchUsers.ts
fetch-users.ts
```

### No whitespace rule
Whitespace in filenames causes URL encoding issues (`%20`) and breaks shell scripts without quoting.

### Checklist
- [ ] No spaces in any filename
- [ ] React components: PascalCase.{tsx,jsx}
- [ ] Hooks: useCamelCase.{ts,js}
- [ ] Utilities: kebab-case.{ts,js}
- [ ] Tests: {name}.test.ts or {name}.spec.ts
- [ ] CSS modules: {ComponentName}.module.css

name: HTML5 Semantic Tags — Meaningful Document Structure
description: Use semantic HTML5 elements instead of div stacks. Improves SEO, accessibility, and screen reader navigation. header/nav/main/article/section/aside/footer.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## HTML5 Semantic Tags ### Tag Reference | Tag | Use when... | |-----|------------| | `
` | Introductory content for page or section; logo, nav, search | | `
` | Primary or secondary navigation links | | `
` | Central content of the page — ONE per page | | `
` | Self-contained content: blog post, product card, news item | | `
` | Thematic grouping of content with a heading | | `
` | Tangentially related content: sidebar, callout, related links | | `
` | Footer for page or section; legal, social links, contact | | `

name: CSS Preprocessor — SASS/LESS Best Practices
description: Use SASS or LESS for variables, mixins, and nesting. Compile to production CSS. Never commit compiled output.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: CSS Global vs Local Style Separation
description: Separate global reset/tokens from component-scoped styles. Use CSS Modules or BEM for local scope. Prevent style leakage.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: CSS Modularization — Essential vs Non-Essential
description: Split CSS into critical (inline) and non-critical (deferred). Load component CSS on demand. Never ship all styles on every page.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## CSS Modularization

### Critical vs Non-Critical
```html
<!-- Critical: inline in <head> (above-fold styles only) -->
<style>/* minimal reset + hero styles */</style>

<!-- Non-critical: load async -->
<link rel="preload" href="/styles/main.css" as="style" onload="this.onload=null;this.rel='stylesheet'">
<noscript><link rel="stylesheet" href="/styles/main.css"></noscript>
```

### Component-level CSS splitting (Next.js / Vite)
```tsx
// Heavy component CSS loads only when component mounts
const DataTable = dynamic(() => import('./DataTable'), { ssr: false });
// DataTable.module.css only loads when DataTable renders
```

### PurgeCSS (remove unused)
```js
// tailwind.config.ts
module.exports = {
  content: ['./src/**/*.{tsx,ts,js}'],
  // Tailwind scans content and removes unused utilities
};
```

### Checklist
- [ ] Above-fold CSS inlined (< 14kb)
- [ ] Non-critical CSS loaded async
- [ ] Unused CSS purged in production build
- [ ] Component CSS lazy-loaded with component
- [ ] Total CSS budget respected (see fg-performance-page-load)

name: CSS Specificity — Be Specific Not Over-Specific
description: Use the minimum specificity needed. Avoid ID selectors, avoid chaining 4+ classes. Low specificity makes styles easier to override and maintain.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## CSS Specificity

### Specificity Scale (lowest to highest)
```
Element selector:        div, p, h1           (0,0,1)
Class / attribute:       .btn, [type=text]    (0,1,0)
ID selector:             #header              (1,0,0)
Inline style:            style=""             (1,0,0,0)
!important:              always wins (avoid)
```

### Rules
- Prefer class selectors over element or ID
- Never use ID selectors for styling (#id)
- Never chain more than 3 classes
- Never use `!important` in component CSS

### WRONG
```css
#sidebar .nav ul li a.active { color: red; }  /* 1,2,2 — nightmare to override */
div.card > div.card-body p.text { font-size: 14px; }  /* over-specific */
```

### CORRECT
```css
.nav-link--active { color: var(--color-accent); }    /* 0,1,0 */
.card__text { font-size: var(--text-sm); }           /* 0,1,0 */
```

### When high specificity is justified
- Scoped CSS (`:where(.component)` wrapper reduces specificity to 0)
- CSS reset overrides for third-party components

### Checklist
- [ ] No ID selectors used for styling
- [ ] Max 3 selectors chained in any rule
- [ ] No `!important` in component styles
- [ ] New styles can be overridden without fighting specificity

name: CSS Browser Rendering — Right-to-Left Selector Matching
description: Browsers match CSS selectors right-to-left. Overly broad selectors cause unnecessary matching across the entire DOM. Write selectors that resolve quickly.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Avoid Inline Styles
description: Don't use style attributes on HTML elements. Inline styles break separation of concerns, can't be overridden by stylesheets, and create maintenance issues.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## Avoid Inline Styles ### Rule Never use `style=""` attributes for static styles. Move to CSS classes. ```html

``` ### Exception: dynamic values from JavaScript Inline styles are acceptable ONLY when the value is computed at runtime: ```tsx // ACCEPTABLE: truly dynamic value

// NOT acceptable: static value masquerading as dynamic
// should be a CSS class ``` ### React: prefer CSS variables for dynamic theming ```tsx // Instead of inline styles for theming
// Then in CSS: color: var(--accent) ``` ### Checklist - [ ] Zero `style=""` attributes in HTML (except truly dynamic values) - [ ] Static visual properties always in CSS class - [ ] Dynamic values use CSS custom properties where possible

name: Avoid !important
description: Never use !important in application CSS. It breaks the cascade, creates specificity debt, and signals a structural CSS problem that should be fixed at the source.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## Avoid !important

### Rule
`!important` is always a symptom. Fix the root cause instead.

### Common root causes and fixes
```css
/* Symptom: can't override third-party library */
.my-btn { color: red !important; } /* WRONG */

/* Fix: increase specificity with wrapper or :where() inverse */
.my-app .my-btn { color: red; }   /* CORRECT: scoped override */

/* Symptom: style wars between components */
.modal h2 { font-size: 1.5rem !important; } /* WRONG */

/* Fix: more specific component selector */
.modal__heading { font-size: 1.5rem; }       /* CORRECT */
```

### When !important is acceptable
- Utility classes designed to always win (e.g., `.sr-only`, `.hidden`, `.text-center`)
- Accessibility override (forced colors, high contrast mode)
- Reset styles for print media

```css
/* Acceptable: intentional utility override */
.sr-only { position: absolute !important; width: 1px !important; clip: rect(0,0,0,0) !important; }
.hidden { display: none !important; }
```

### Finding !important in codebase
```bash
grep -rn '!important' src/styles/ | grep -v 'sr-only\|hidden\|print'
```

### Checklist
- [ ] No `!important` in component CSS files
- [ ] Third-party overrides use scoped selectors
- [ ] Utility classes are the only permitted `!important` users
- [ ] CI grep for rogue `!important` additions

name: CSS Consistency & Methodology (BEM/ITCSS/OOCSS)
description: Pick one CSS methodology and apply it consistently across the entire codebase. BEM for component naming, ITCSS for file organization, OOCSS for reuse.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Use a Design System & CSS Variables
description: Define all design tokens as CSS custom properties. Use a design system (Tailwind, Material, custom) consistently. Never hardcode colors, spacing, or typography.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Use Relative Units (em/rem/vw/vh/%)
description: Prefer relative units over px. rem for typography and spacing, % and vw/vh for layout. Enables accessible scaling and fluid responsive design.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## Relative CSS Units

### Unit Guide
| Unit | Use for | Relative to |
|------|---------|------------|
| `rem` | Font size, spacing | Root font size (16px default) |
| `em` | Padding proportional to element's font | Element's own font-size |
| `%` | Width, max-width | Parent container |
| `vw/vh` | Hero heights, full-bleed layouts | Viewport |
| `clamp()` | Fluid typography, fluid spacing | Viewport + bounds |
| `px` | Borders, shadows, 1px lines | Fixed — use sparingly |

### Fluid Typography with clamp()
```css
:root {
  /* Scales from 1rem (320px viewport) to 1.125rem (1440px viewport) */
  --text-base: clamp(1rem, 0.92rem + 0.36vw, 1.125rem);
  
  /* Hero: 2.5rem on mobile → 5rem on desktop */
  --text-hero: clamp(2.5rem, 1.5rem + 5vw, 5rem);
}
```

### Spacing scale in rem
```css
/* 4px grid — 1rem = 16px base */
:root {
  --space-1: 0.25rem;  /* 4px */
  --space-2: 0.5rem;   /* 8px */
  --space-4: 1rem;     /* 16px */
  --space-8: 2rem;     /* 32px */
}
```

### Accessibility: why not px for font-size
If user sets browser font size to 20px (accessibility), `rem`-based layouts scale correctly. `px`-based layouts ignore user preference.

### Checklist
- [ ] No `px` for font-size (use rem)
- [ ] No `px` for spacing (use rem or CSS variable)
- [ ] Fluid typography uses `clamp()` for responsive scaling
- [ ] Layout uses `%`, `vw/vh`, `fr` for fluid widths
- [ ] `px` only for borders and shadows

name: Avoid Expensive CSS Properties in Animations
description: Only animate compositor-friendly properties (transform, opacity, clip-path). Animating layout properties (width, height, top, left) causes layout thrash and dropped frames.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Don't Remove outline — Style It
description: Never use outline:none or outline:0 without a replacement. Focus indicators are critical for keyboard navigation and WCAG compliance. Style the outline instead.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Responsive or Fluid Styling
description: Design for all screen sizes. Use CSS Grid, Flexbox, and clamp() for fluid layouts. Media queries for discrete breakpoints. Mobile-first approach.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Font Loading Strategy
description: Preload critical font files. Use font-display: swap. Subset fonts. Max two font families. Avoid FOUT and layout shift from font swap.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Minimize & Validate CSS (PurgeCSS, PostCSS)
description: Remove unused CSS in production. Validate CSS syntax. Run PostCSS for autoprefixer and optimization. Reduces CSS payload and eliminates cross-browser issues.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Explicit Transition Properties
description: Always name specific properties in transition declarations. Never use 'transition: all'. Transitioning all properties causes unexpected repaints and degrades performance.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Avoid Constantly Overwriting Styles
description: Don't set a style then immediately override it. Reveals a structural CSS problem. Use modifiers, variants, or conditional class application instead.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Turn Repeated Tricks into Utility Classes
description: When the same CSS pattern repeats across 3+ components, extract it into a utility class. Builds a shared vocabulary for the team.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Box Sizing border-box Global Reset
description: Always apply box-sizing: border-box globally. Without it, padding and borders add to element dimensions, making layout calculations unpredictable.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Avoid id Attributes for Styling
description: Never use #id selectors in stylesheets. IDs have high specificity, can't be reused, and couple HTML structure to CSS. Use class selectors instead.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## Avoid ID Attributes for Styling ### Rule ID attributes have three valid uses: 1. Fragment links (`` + `
`) 2. ARIA associations (`
❌ can't reuse
✅ reusable ``` ### Correct ID usage (non-styling) ```html About Us
``` ### Audit ```bash grep -rn '^#[a-z]' src/styles/ --include='*.css' --include='*.scss' ``` ### Checklist - [ ] Zero `#id` selectors in CSS/SCSS files - [ ] IDs used only for ARIA, fragments, and JS hooks - [ ] All styling done via class selectors

name: React Folder Structure — Feature-Based Organization
description: Organize React code by feature, not by file type. Each feature has its own directory with components, hooks, and styles co-located.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
template:
## React Feature-Based Folder Structure

### Recommended Structure
```
src/
├── app/                     # App shell, providers, router
│   ├── App.tsx
│   ├── Router.tsx
│   └── providers/
├── features/                # Feature modules
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   │   ├── LoginForm.tsx
│   │   │   └── LoginForm.test.tsx
│   │   ├── hooks/
│   │   │   └── useAuth.ts
│   │   ├── api/
│   │   │   └── authApi.ts
│   │   └── index.ts          # public API
│   └── dashboard/
│       ├── components/
│       ├── hooks/
│       └── index.ts
├── shared/                  # Truly shared across features
│   ├── components/
│   │   ├── Button/
│   │   │   ├── Button.tsx
│   │   │   ├── Button.module.css
│   │   │   └── Button.test.tsx
│   │   └── Modal/
│   ├── hooks/
│   │   ├── useDebounce.ts
│   │   └── useLocalStorage.ts
│   └── lib/
│       ├── api-client.ts
│       └── format-date.ts
└── styles/
    ├── tokens.css
    ├── global.css
    └── utilities.css
```

### Import rules
- Features can import from `shared/`
- Features CANNOT import from other features directly
- Cross-feature communication via state management or events
- `index.ts` exports the public API of each feature

### Checklist
- [ ] Code organized by feature, not by file type
- [ ] Tests co-located with source files
- [ ] Each feature has an `index.ts` public API
- [ ] Cross-feature imports go through `shared/` or state
- [ ] No `components/` at root with 40+ unrelated files

name: React Component Definition — Directory with index.tsx
description: Define each non-trivial React component in its own directory with index.tsx as the entry point. Co-locate styles, tests, and sub-components.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: React Code Standards — Hooks, SRP, Context
description: Write React components following hooks-first, single responsibility, and context-for-shared-state principles. Avoid class components, prop drilling, and god components.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: TypeScript in React — No any, Strict Mode
description: All React components and hooks must be TypeScript. Enable strict mode. Never use any. Type all props, return types for hooks, and API responses.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: React State Management — Context First, Redux Last
description: Choose state management based on scope and complexity. useState for local, Context for feature-wide, Zustand/Jotai for app-wide, Redux only for large complex apps.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: React Performance — useCallback/useMemo Judiciously
description: Don't prematurely memoize. Profile first. useMemo and useCallback only where measured render cost justifies the overhead. React.memo for expensive pure components.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Next.js — SSR, App Router, Server Components
description: Use Next.js App Router with React Server Components for data-fetching. Reserve client components for interactivity. Optimize with SSG where possible.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: React Error Boundaries & Error Handling
description: Wrap feature sections in error boundaries. Handle async errors with error states. Never let uncaught errors crash the entire app.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi

name: Code Splitting — React.lazy + Suspense
description: Split code by route and feature using React.lazy and Suspense. Never load the entire application bundle upfront.
source: Frontend Development Guidelines v1.0.0 — Sachidananda Panigrahi
